I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.", "date": "August 01, 2016 at 03:44 AM"}, {"review_id": "reviewid_1067", "user_id": "userid_213", "business_ref": "businessref_89", "rating": "5", "useful": "2", "funny": "0", "cool": "0", "text": "Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import re

# Business data from var_functions.query_db:26
business_records = var_functions.query_db_26

def extract_clean_categories(description):
    """Extract categories from business description, cleaning location and other noise"""
    
    # Remove location info (everything after first period or at first "Located at")
    desc_cleaned = description.split('.')[0]
    
    # Convert to lower for easier parsing
    desc_lower = desc_cleaned.lower()
    
    # Find category section based on keyword markers
    markers = ['services in', 'services including', 'premier destination for']
    categories_text = ""
    
    for marker in markers:
        if marker in desc_lower:
            start_idx = desc_lower.index(marker) + len(marker)
            categories_text = desc_cleaned[start_idx:].strip()
            break
    
    if not categories_text:
        return []
    
    # Clean up
    categories_text = categories_text.strip().rstrip('.')
    
    # Split by commas and handle "and"
    categories = []
    for item in categories_text.split(','):
        item = item.strip()
        
        # Split "and" separated items
        if ' and ' in item:
            subitems = item.split(' and ')
            for subitem in subitems:
                subitem = subitem.strip()
                if subitem and not 'Located' in subitem:
                    categories.append(subitem)
        else:
            if item and not 'Located' in item:
                categories.append(item)
    
    # Title case and clean up each category
    cleaned_categories = []
    for cat in categories:
        # Remove leading/trailing spaces and artifacts
        cat = cat.strip()
        if cat.startswith('and '):
            cat = cat[4:]
        if cat and len(cat) > 2:  # Skip very short artifacts
            cleaned_categories.append(cat.title())
    
    return cleaned_categories

# Map business_id to categories
business_to_categories = {}
for record in business_records:
    business_id = record['business_id']
    description = record['description']
    categories = extract_clean_categories(description)
    business_to_categories[business_id] = categories

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(business_to_categories, indent=2))

    desc_lower = desc_cleaned.lower()
    
    # Find category section based on keyword markers
    markers = ['services in', 'services including', 'premier destination for']
    categories_text = ""
    
    for marker in markers:
        if marker in desc_lower:
            start_idx = desc_lower.index(marker) + len(marker)
            categories_text = desc_cleaned[start_idx:].strip()
            break
    
    if not categories_text:
        return []
    
    # Clean up
    categories_text = categories_text.strip().rstrip('.')
    
    # Split by commas and handle "and"
    categories = []
    for item in categories_text.split(','):
        item = item.strip()
        
        # Split "and" separated items
        if ' and ' in item:
            subitems = item.split(' and ')
            for subitem in subitems:
                subitem = subitem.strip()
                if subitem and not 'Located' in subitem:
                    categories.append(subitem)
        else:
            if item and not 'Located' in item:
                categories.append(item)
    
    # Title case and clean up each category
    cleaned_categories = []
    for cat in categories:
        # Remove leading/trailing spaces and artifacts
        cat = cat.strip()
        if cat.startswith('and '):
            cat = cat[4:]
        if cat and len(cat) > 2:  # Skip very short artifacts
            cleaned_categories.append(cat.title())
    
    return cleaned_categories

# Map business_id to categories
business_to_categories = {}
for record in business_records:
    business_id = record['business_id']
    description = record['description']
    categories = extract_clean_categories(description)
    business_to_categories[business_id] = categories

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(business_to_categories, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get business records
business_records = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Function to extract categories from description
def extract_categories(description):
    # Remove location prefix if present
    desc = re.sub(r'^Located at [^\.]+\.\s*', '', description)
    
    # Find the category list (look for common patterns)
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including ([^.]+)',
        r'offers enthusiasts a premier destination for ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services including ([^.]+)'
    ]
    
    categories_text = ""
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_text = match.group(1)
            break
    
    if not categories_text:
        return []
    
    # Split by comma and "and"
    categories = []
    parts = re.split(r',|\band\b', categories_text)
    
    for part in parts:
        part = part.strip()
        # Skip empty parts and location info
        if part and not part.startswith('Located at'):
            # Clean up the category name
            part = re.sub(r'^including\s+', '', part, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
            part = re.sub(r'^such as\s+', '', part, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
            categories.append(part.title())
    
    return categories

# Map business_id (without prefix) to categories
business_to_categories = {}
for record in business_records:
    business_id_full = record['business_id']  # e.g., businessid_49
    business_id_num = business_id_full.split('_')[1]  # e.g., 49
    description = record['description']
    categories = extract_categories(description)
    business_to_categories[business_id_num] = categories

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(business_to_categories, indent=2))

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load data from previous queries
business_records = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Get all reviews for 2016 users from 2016 onwards
review_file = var_functions_query_db_42
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_records = json.load(f)

# Function to extract categories from business description
def extract_categories_from_description(description):
    """Extract business categories from description text"""
    # Find the category list in description
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including ([^.]+)',
        r'offers enthusiasts a premier destination for ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services including ([^.]+)'
    ]
    
    categories_text = ""
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_text = match.group(1)
            break
    
    if not categories_text:
        return []
    
    # Split by commas and "and", clean up each category
    categories = []
    parts = re.split(r',|, and |\band\b', categories_text)
    
    for part in parts:
        part = part.strip()
        if part and len(part) > 2:
            # Clean up common artifacts
            part = part.rstrip('.').strip()
            categories.append(part.title())
    
    return categories

# Map business_id (businessref_ format) to categories
business_to_categories = {}
for record in business_records:
    business_id_num = record['business_id'].split('_')[1]  # e.g., "49"
    business_ref = f'businessref_{business_id_num}'  # Convert to businessref_ format
    description = record['description']
    categories = extract_categories_from_description(description)
    # Remove empty categories and duplicates
    categories = [cat for cat in categories if cat and cat != 'And']
    business_to_categories[business_ref] = categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_count = Counter()

for review in review_records:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_to_categories:
        categories = business_to_categories[business_ref]
        for category in categories:
            category_review_count[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_count.most_common(5)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(top_5_categories, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import re
from collections import Counter

business_records = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Load review data from file
review_file = "/tmp/tmp6y8b0d4m.json"
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_records = json.load(f)

# Function to extract categories from business description
def extract_categories(description):
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including ([^.]+)',
        r'offers enthusiasts a premier destination for ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services including ([^.]+)'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma or 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',| and ', text)
            return [p.strip().title() for p in parts if p.strip()]
    
    return []

# Map business_ref to categories
business_category_map = {}
for record in business_records:
    business_id_num = record['business_id'].split('_')[1]
    business_ref = 'businessref_' + business_id_num
    categories = extract_categories(record['description'])
    # Filter out problematic categories
    categories = [cat for cat in categories if cat != 'And']
    business_category_map[business_ref] = categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
for review in review_records:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_category_map:
        for category in business_category_map[business_ref]:
            category_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top5 = dict(category_counts.most_common(5))

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(top5, indent=2))

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Get business records from storage
business_records = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Get review records from storage - file path stored in variable
review_data_var = 'var_functions.query_db:42'
review_file_path = locals()[review_data_var]

# Load all review records
with open(review_file_path, 'r') as f:
    review_records = json.load(f)

# Function to extract categories from business description
def extract_categories_from_description(description):
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including ([^.]+)',
        r'offers enthusiasts a premier destination for ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services including ([^.]+)'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma or 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',| and ', text)
            # Clean and return categories
            return [p.strip().title() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip().lower() != 'and']
    
    return []

# Map business_ref to categories
business_category_map = {}
for record in business_records:
    business_id_num = record['business_id'].split('_')[1]
    business_ref = 'businessref_' + business_id_num
    description = record['description']
    categories = extract_categories_from_description(description)
    business_category_map[business_ref] = categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = Counter()

for review in review_records:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_category_map:
        categories = business_category_map[business_ref]
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_counts.most_common(5)

# Format result
result = {category: count for category, count in top_5_categories}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Get business records from storage
business_records = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Get review records from storage - file path stored in variable
review_data_var = 'var_functions.query_db:42'
review_file_path = locals()[review_data_var]

# Load all review records
with open(review_file_path, 'r') as f:
    review_records = json.load(f)

# Function to extract categories from business description
def extract_categories_from_description(description):
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including ([^.]+)',
        r'offers enthusiasts a premier destination for ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services in ([^.]+)',
        r'offers a range of services including ([^.]+)'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma or 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',| and ', text)
            # Clean and return categories
            return [p.strip().title() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip().lower() != 'and']
    
    return []

# Map business_ref to categories
business_category_map = {}
for record in business_records:
    business_id_num = record['business_id'].split('_')[1]
    business_ref = 'businessref_' + business_id_num
    description = record['description']
    categories = extract_categories_from_description(description)
    business_category_map[business_ref] = categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = Counter()

for review in review_records:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_category_map:
        categories = business_category_map[business_ref]
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_counts.most_common(5)

# Format result
result = {category: count for category, count in top_5_categories}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load business data
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:52']

# Load review data from file
review_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:50']
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

# Function to extract categories from business description
def extract_categories(description):
    """Extract categories from business description text"""
    # Remove location info (text before first period)
    desc_cleaned = description.split('.')[0] if '.' in description else description
    
    # Find categories after common phrases
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in (.+)',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+)',
        r'offers enthusiasts a premier destination for (.+)',
        r'offers a range of services in (.+)',
        r'offers a range of services including (.+)'
    ]
    
    categories_text = ""
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_cleaned, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_text = match.group(1)
            break
    
    if not categories_text:
        return []
    
    # Split by comma and "and", then clean up
    categories = []
    # Split on comma or "and" 
    parts = re.split(r',| and ', categories_text)
    
    for part in parts:
        part = part.strip()
        # Skip empty parts
        if not part:
            continue
        # Remove trailing period
        part = part.rstrip('.')
        # Skip if it's just "and"
        if part.lower() == 'and':
            continue
        # Add to categories
        categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Create mapping from business_ref to categories
business_ref_to_categories = {}
for business in business_data:
    business_id = business['business_id']  # e.g., businessid_49
    business_num = business_id.split('_')[1]  # e.g., 49
    business_ref = f'businessref_{business_num}'  # e.g., businessref_49
    
    description = business['description']
    categories = extract_categories(description)
    business_ref_to_categories[business_ref] = categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = Counter()

for review in review_data:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_ref_to_categories:
        categories = business_ref_to_categories[business_ref]
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_counts.most_common(5)

# Format output
result = {
    "top_5_categories": [
        {"category": cat, "review_count": count} 
        for cat, count in top_5_categories
    ]
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load business data
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:52']

# Load review data from file
review_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:50']
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

# Function to extract categories from business description
def extract_categories(description):
    # Remove location info (text before first period)
    desc_cleaned = description.split('.')[0] if '.' in description else description
    
    # Find categories after common phrases
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in (.+)',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+)',
        r'offers enthusiasts a premier destination for (.+)',
        r'offers a range of services in (.+)',
        r'offers a range of services including (.+)'
    ]
    
    categories_text = ''
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_cleaned, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_text = match.group(1)
            break
    
    if not categories_text:
        return []
    
    # Split by comma and 'and', then clean up
    categories = []
    parts = re.split(r',| and ', categories_text)
    
    for part in parts:
        part = part.strip()
        if not part:
            continue
        part = part.rstrip('.')
        if part.lower() == 'and':
            continue
        categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Create mapping from business_ref to categories
business_ref_to_categories = {}
for business in business_data:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_num = business_id.split('_')[1]
    business_ref = 'businessref_' + business_num
    
    description = business['description']
    categories = extract_categories(description)
    business_ref_to_categories[business_ref] = categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = Counter()

for review in review_data:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if business_ref in business_ref_to_categories:
        categories = business_ref_to_categories[business_ref]
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_counts.most_common(5)

# Format output
result = []
for cat, count in top_5_categories:
    result.append({'category': cat, 'review_count': count})

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
